document.title: https://www.howtogeek.com/why-i-do-not-use-deepin-linux/
document.raw_content:
Did you recently lay your eyes on Deepin Linux and think to yourself: this is the best-looking Linux distro ever—why don't more people recommend it? Well, I, too, agree that Deepin is beautiful, but it's plagued with too many security issues. That's one of the reasons why I'll never use it, and why I recommend others avoid it too.
The allure of Deepin Linux
Beautiful, polished, and literally too good to be true
Deepin is, without a question, one of the most visually stunning Linux distros. The entire interface is built around a glossy, glass-like aesthetic with translucency effects and subtle animations that make every interaction feel smooth and premium. It doesn't look like a hobby project thrown together by enthusiasts—it looks like something you'd see on a work computer at a design agency.
The default layout mirrors Windows 11, with a bottom panel containing all the pinned apps centralized around the middle section. However, you can easily switch it to look more like Windows 7, if you prefer the left-side alignment. It also comes loaded with a bunch of pre-built themes. With a single click, you can change the wallpaper, color scheme, icon pack, and the transparency level. It looks incredible—but as with most things that seem too good to be true, there's always a catch.
5 best Windows-like Linux desktops for a familiar look and feel
Linux doesn't have to look like Linux—and these five desktops prove it.
OpenSUSE developers claim DDE is bypassing their security policies
They were ignoring years of warnings
The reason Deepin looks nothing like most Linux distros is because it’s using their own desktop environment, aptly called the Deepin Desktop Environment (DDE). When you praise Deepin for its good looks, you’re actually praising DDE. In fact, you can install DDE on any Linux distro, and it’ll look just as dazzling. However, some Linux distros discourage using DDE—and for good reason.
For instance, in May 2025, openSUSE—one of the most well-established Linux distributions—was no longer officially supporting DDE. The decision came after years of frustration with security vulnerabilities found in DDE's core components, with problems dating as far back as 2017. The openSUSE security team had repeatedly flagged these issues to Deepin's developers, only to receive slow responses, inadequate fixes, or in some cases, no response at all.
The final straw came when the security team discovered that someone had quietly slipped in a package back in 2021 that allowed it to bypass openSUSE's mandatory security review process altogether—essentially installing components that were supposed to be flagged and scrutinized, without anyone on the security team ever knowing about it. Even the Arch Linux Wiki—which typically just presents information and lets users make their own calls—flags this at the very top of its DDE page, directly quoting openSUSE's assessment.
There are trackers in the distro
It’s a different kind of pain when you see spyware on Linux
The openSUSE situation isn't the first time Deepin has raised red flags. Back in 2018, a YouTuber named quidsup was monitoring his network traffic and noticed that Deepin's app store was quietly sending data to CNZZ—a Chinese analytics service similar to Google Analytics. What made it particularly alarming wasn't just the tracking itself, but the fact that it was happening in the background even when you weren't actively using the app store.
Now, following the backlash, they made a public statement that CNZZ was only collecting anonymous usage data—things like screen resolution and browser information—and not personal data. However, a big part of Linux’s appeal is privacy and control over your system. If I wanted my operating system to gather telemetry or track my usage, I would’ve kept using Windows.
Overall, as the openSUSE developers put it, Deepin comes off as a company “lacking security culture.” First they had trackers. Then they don’t do proper security reviews and try to bypass (read: cheat) the system. I’m not comfortable trusting a company that is this lackadaisical about security. Sure, Deepin and DDE are both open source, meaning you can review the code yourself, but the codebase of an entire operating system is massive—auditing it to ensure everything checks out is a lot of work and impractical.
This is exactly why I always recommend installing Portmaster on your system. It’s FOSS, easy-to-use, and gives you a graphical app to see which applications are sending data, where it's going—and the power to block anything that looks suspicious.
So, what to do if you like Deepin’s aesthetics?
When you don’t like the default look—theme it to look better
Credit where it's due—Deepin's developers have built something genuinely beautiful. However, a stunning operating system with a poor security track record is a bad trade, and there are better options out there that don't ask you to make that compromise.
For example, if you like the Deepin aesthetics, you can potentially recreate much of it using a KDE Plasma-based Linux distro. The desktop environment gives you granular control over almost every interface element. On top of that, you get access to a rich collection of themes that can completely change how the desktop looks and feels. For instance, PearOS uses a customized version of KDE Plasma with transparent theming that looks on par with macOS.
Security and stability are far more important than looks when it comes to evaluating an operating system. It's the foundation on which you store your files and run all your apps. If it's compromised, your entire digital life is compromised. Until Deepin's developers take security seriously, this distro ranks dead last on my recommendation list.

findings.suggestion: Treat this as likely human-authored; consider checking author byline or publication metadata for confirmation.

anchors.quote: Did you recently lay your eyes on Deepin Linux and think to yourself: this is the best-looking Linux distro ever—why don't more people recommend it? Well, I, too, agree that Deepin is beautiful, but it's plagued with too many security issues.

For instance, in May 2025, openSUSE—one of the most well-established Linux distributions—was no longer officially supporting DDE.
